serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Garlic Mutton Masala in a Singaporean hawker or restaurant context is approximately 200 g, served as a main dish alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Garlic Mutton Masala — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Garlic Mutton Masala (cr_id G00010862). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB record and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked mutton (USDA NDB 17175) as a supplementary reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Garlic Mutton Masala. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous mixed meat curry with onion-tomato gravy from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with only 7.44 g carbohydrate per 100 g, primarily from onion, tomato, and spices, which are low-GI ingredients. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (35 × 14.88 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 5.2 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467 mg per 100 g; a 200 g serving delivers approximately 934 mg sodium, representing 41% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced-salt preparation; garlic's mild vasodilatory effect provides a modest benefit but does not offset the high sodium load at standard serving sizes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate at 7.44 g per 100 g and high protein at 14.05 g per 100 g produce a low glycemic response; estimated GI of 35 places this dish in the low-GI category.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Garlic Mutton Masala is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in a 150–200 g portion without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; garlic's allicin content has evidence-based insulin-sensitising properties that provide an additional modest glycaemic benefit.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 11.58 g per 100 g with estimated saturated fat approximately 4.8 g per 100 g and cholesterol 85 mg per 100 g; high sodium further elevates cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to one small serving (100–150 g) per occasion, choose lean mutton cuts, and pair with high-fibre vegetables to partially offset the saturated fat and sodium burden; garlic's anti-platelet and lipid-modulating properties provide a modest cardioprotective benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is a red meat with high arachidonic acid (omega-6) content that amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways relevant to RA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise mutton consumption and consider substituting with chicken or legumes; garlic's anti-inflammatory polyphenols are beneficial and should be retained in any substituted preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Garlic Mutton Masala is a Singaporean Indian dish consisting of tender chunks of mutton (goat meat) slow-cooked in a richly spiced onion-tomato gravy with a prominent garlic base. The dish is prepared by first sautéing sliced onions and whole garlic cloves in oil until golden, then adding tomatoes, ground masala spices (typically coriander, cumin, chilli, turmeric, and garam masala), and mutton pieces, which are braised until the meat is tender and the gravy is thick and aromatic. The flavour profile is deeply savoury, mildly spicy, and pungent from the garlic, with a slight sweetness from the caramelised onions and tomatoes. The texture features soft, yielding meat in a semi-thick, oil-glossed gravy. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 192 kcal, 14.05 g protein, 11.58 g fat, and 7.44 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. Sodium is elevated at 467 mg per 100 g, which is a concern for hypertension management. The estimated glycemic index is 35 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes when not paired with high-GI staples. Garlic contributes allicin and organosulfur compounds with evidence-based anti-inflammatory, insulin-sensitising, and mild vasodilatory properties. The dish is commonly served at Singaporean Indian hawker stalls and restaurants as a main course with rice or roti, and is popular at lunch and dinner. It is halal-certified in most Singaporean contexts.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010862) at confidence 0.95 with exact match. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked mutton as a supplementary reference and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, clearly documented.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific Singaporean garlic mutton masala preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry (G00010862) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for an exact equivalent composite dish as of 2026. Saturated fat estimated at 4.8 g per 100 g from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked mutton (NDB 17175) as the closest approved-source analogue; this is documented as an adaptation. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on mixed meat curry with onion-tomato gravy from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in any approved source. Halal status is assumed based on the dish's common preparation in Singaporean Indian Muslim hawker contexts; individual vendor certification should be verified. The dish contains no dairy and no gluten-containing ingredients in standard preparation.
